title: Sterile & Non-Sterile Compounding — USP 795/797/800
description: Non-sterile compounding (USP 795), sterile compounding (USP 797), hazardous drugs (USP 800), beyond-use dating, and cleanroom requirements.
subject: pharmacy

cards.front: USP <795> vs <797> vs <800>: scope of each chapter
cards.back: USP <795>: Non-sterile compounding (ointments, capsules, oral solutions). USP <797>: Sterile compounding (injections, eye drops, IV admixtures). USP <800>: Hazardous drug handling (applies to both sterile and non-sterile). USP <825>: Radiopharmaceuticals

cards.front: Beyond-use date (BUD) vs expiration date: distinction
cards.back: Expiration date: manufacturer-assigned date based on stability testing of original unopened product. BUD: date assigned by pharmacist/compounder after compounding or opening — based on USP guidelines and stability data. BUD ≤ expiration date

cards.front: USP <797> BUD for Category 1 sterile preparations (no sterility testing)
cards.back: Category 1 CSPs (compounded sterile preparations): BUD ≤ 12 hours at controlled room temperature (CRT) OR ≤ 24 hours at refrigerated (36–46°F/2–8°C). Applies to high-risk preparations made without full ISO 5 primary engineering controls

cards.front: USP <797> BUD for Category 2 sterile preparations
cards.back: Category 2 CSPs with sterility and endotoxin testing: CRT: up to 45 days. Refrigerated: up to 90 days. Frozen: up to 1 year. Without full testing but using ISO 5 PEC: shorter limits apply. Category 2 requires ISO 7 buffer room + ISO 5 PEC minimum

cards.front: ISO cleanroom classifications: particle count standards
cards.back: ISO 5: ≤ 3,520 particles/m³ (≥0.5 μm) — primary engineering control (laminar flow hood, RABS, isolator). ISO 7: ≤ 352,000 particles/m³ — buffer room. ISO 8: ≤ 3,520,000 particles/m³ — ante room. 'Cleaner' = lower ISO number

cards.front: Primary engineering controls (PECs): types and uses
cards.back: LAFW (laminar airflow workbench): positive pressure, for non-hazardous sterile compounding. BSC (biological safety cabinet): negative pressure, for hazardous drugs (USP 800). CACI (compounding aseptic containment isolator): for hazardous sterile. RABS: restricted-access barrier system

cards.front: Garbing order for sterile compounding (outside to inside)
cards.back: Outside ante room: shoe covers, hair cover, face mask. Ante room: hand washing, gown. Buffer room: sterile gloves (after hand hygiene). The 'outside-in' order prevents contaminating clean areas. Gloves = last item applied in cleanroom

cards.front: USP <800>: scope and who must comply
cards.back: Applies to ALL healthcare settings that receive, store, compound, or administer hazardous drugs (HDs). Includes hospitals, outpatient pharmacies, clinics, physician offices. HDs list based on NIOSH table. Applies to non-sterile and sterile HD compounding

Group 1: antineoplastic agents (highest hazard). Group 2: non-antineoplastic HDs (reproductive risks, organ toxicity). Group 3: drugs that meet HD criteria for reproductive hazard only. Examples: methotrexate, cyclophosphamide, warfarin (Group 3), some hormones (estrogens, finasteride)

Required for preparing and administering antineoplastic agents per USP <800>. CSTDs prevent escape of HD vapor and liquid (examples: PhaSeal, Equashield, ChemoClave). Must be used when HDs are dissolved or volatilize at room temperature

cards.front: Master formulation record vs compounding record: distinction
cards.back: Master formulation record (MFR): the 'recipe' — contains formulation, ingredients, equipment, BUD, reference sources. Compounding record (CR): the 'batch record' — documents each specific batch made (lot numbers, quantities used, pharmacist verification, final checks)

Non-aqueous (oils, ointments): up to 6 months or 70% of remaining API shelf life. Aqueous liquids (oral solutions, suspensions): ≤ 14 days refrigerated (no preservative) — 35 days maximum with established stability. Solid preparations (capsules): ≤ 6 months. Topical aqueous: ≤ 30 days

USP sterility test requires 14 days incubation at 20–25°C (aerobic) and 30–35°C (anaerobic). Time needed for slow-growing microorganisms to produce visible turbidity. Rapid testing alternatives now available but require USP validation

Limulus amebocyte lysate (LAL) test detects bacterial endotoxins (gram-negative). Maximum endotoxin limit (MEL): 5 EU/kg/hr for parenteral drugs (general). CNS route: 0.2 EU/kg/hr. Water for injection must pass LAL test. Rabbit pyrogen test used when LAL is not applicable

cards.front: Hand hygiene for sterile compounding: soap vs ABHR
cards.back: Wash hands ≥ 30 seconds with soap and water first (removes gross contamination). Then apply alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) — more effective against broad spectrum of organisms. In ISO buffer room: sterile gloves are required AND hand hygiene must be performed

Oleaginous (hydrocarbon): Petrolatum — occlusive, anhydrous, won't absorb water. Absorption: Aquaphor — anhydrous but can absorb water. W/O emulsion: cold cream — holds water-soluble drugs. O/W emulsion: Hydrophilic ointment — water-washable, non-greasy (preferred for cosmetics)

Fatty/cocoa butter (oleaginous): melts at body temp (34–36°C), avoid overheating. PEG (polyethylene glycol): dissolves in rectal fluid, no melting needed, keep moisture-free. Glycerinated gelatin: less common, primarily vaginal use. Calculate dose displacement value when making suppositories

Trituration: dry grinding of solids to reduce particle size and mix evenly (mortar and pestle). Levigation: adding a small amount of levigating agent (same base or compatible oil) to wet-grind an insoluble powder before incorporating into ointment → prevents gritty texture

When mixing drugs of unequal quantities: add equal volume of diluent/base to small drug, mix thoroughly; then add another equal volume and mix again, repeat until all is incorporated. Prevents uneven distribution of potent low-dose drugs. Required for capsule filling with low-dose actives

Used for hand-filling hard gelatin capsules: spread powder blend on smooth surface, use capsule body to repeatedly punch/scoop powder until filled. Pack consistently for uniform weight. Quality check: weigh 10 capsules, calculate mean weight and individual deviations

Polyethylene glycol (PEG) base most common (hard candy texture). Chocolate base available. Drugs must be heat-stable (molds require melting). Used for: hormones (BHRT), antibiotics (clindamycin), antifungals (nystatin), hormone therapy, buprenorphine. BUD: 30–35 days

Lipophilic drugs: penetrate skin better; use petrolatum or lipophilic base. Hydrophilic drugs: require penetration enhancers (DMSO, propylene glycol) or specialized bases (PLO — pluronic lecithin organogel). Transdermal absorption highly variable; not well-studied for many compounded drugs

WFI is purified water that meets USP specifications: free of pyrogens (endotoxin < 0.25 EU/mL), low microbial burden, produced by distillation or reverse osmosis. Required for parenteral products. Sterile WFI (in sealed vials) used for injection and reconstitution

Standard cycle: 121°C, 15 psi, 15–30 minutes. Validates by biological indicators (Geobacillus stearothermophilus spores — most resistant to moist heat). Used for: glassware, media, heat-stable solutions. NOT for heat-labile drugs (proteins, some APIs)

cards.front: Dry heat sterilization: temperature and use
cards.back: 160–170°C for 2+ hours. Kills both organisms and endotoxins (depyrogenation: 250°C for 30 min). Used for: glassware, oils, waxes, anhydrous powders. Does NOT penetrate moisture. Validated with Bacillus atrophaeus spores

cards.front: Filtration sterilization: pore size and use
cards.back: 0.22 μm (or 0.2 μm) filter: sterilizing grade — removes bacteria and fungi but NOT viruses or prions. Used for heat-labile solutions (some biologics, certain APIs). 0.45 μm: prefiltration, clarifying. Integrity testing (bubble point) required to verify filter before and after use

Viable air sampling (impaction): active sampling with agar plates. Surface sampling: contact plates and swabs. Non-viable particle counting. Personnel glove fingertip sampling. Frequency depends on ISO classification and risk level. Failure triggers investigation and corrective action

HEPA (high-efficiency particulate air): filters ≥ 99.97% of particles ≥ 0.3 μm. Used in laminar airflow workbenches, BSCs, isolators. Must be certified annually (IEST standards). Directional airflow: horizontal LAFW blows toward operator; vertical LAFW blows downward

503A: traditional pharmacy compounding for individual patients with valid prescription. State Board of Pharmacy regulated. 503B: outsourcing facilities — can compound without patient-specific Rx, must follow cGMP, FDA regulated. Must register with FDA, pay fees, subject to inspection

cards.front: Flavoring agents for pediatric and veterinary compounding
cards.back: Key principles: consider drug properties (e.g., sweet base for bitter drugs). Common pediatric flavors: grape, bubblegum, strawberry. Common veterinary: chicken, bacon, fish, beef. Sweeteners: sucrose (cariogenic), sorbitol, stevia, saccharin. Avoid sorbitol in large quantities (osmotic diarrhea)

Example: Amoxicillin 250 mg/5 mL: vial contains 2,500 mg powder. Add 100 mL water → final volume = 100 mL (powder volume negligible, or add to 85 mL for 100 mL final). C = 2,500/100 = 25 mg/mL = 250 mg/10 mL. Shake well, label with BUD (usually 7–14 days refrigerated)

cards.front: Parenteral route compatibility issues: Y-site and piggyback
cards.back: Y-site compatibility: two drugs run simultaneously through same IV line at Y-junction — must be physically and chemically compatible. Check compatibility reference (Trissel's, King Guide). Incompatible signs: precipitate, color change, turbidity. Critical: phenytoin (precipitates in glucose)

cards.front: pH and drug stability in solution
cards.back: Most drugs have optimal pH range for stability. Acidic drugs (penicillins): stable at low pH, degrade in alkaline. Basic drugs: stable at higher pH. IV bags of phenytoin: must be diluted in NS (not D5W) — lower pH of NS better for stability; glucose causes precipitation

cards.front: Bioavailability enhancement in compounding: particle size reduction
cards.back: Micronization: reduces API particle size to 1–10 μm → ↑ surface area → ↑ dissolution rate → ↑ bioavailability. Used for poorly water-soluble drugs (progesterone, griseofulvin). Nano-formulation and amorphous solid dispersions are further enhancements

Each compound requires: master formulation record (MFR) and compounding record (CR). MFR: formulation, equipment, reference, BUD rationale. CR: name, date, lot numbers, quantities weighed, compounder and pharmacist initials, quality checks. Retained per state regulations (usually 2–5 years)

cards.front: Overage/overages in compounding: definition and limit
cards.back: Overages: excess API added to a compounded preparation to account for losses during manufacturing or to ensure labeled amount throughout BUD. Must be justified and documented. Typically ≤ 10% of nominal dose. Excessive overage without justification is considered adulteration

cards.front: Solubility enhancement techniques in non-sterile compounding
cards.back: Cosolvents: add ethanol, propylene glycol, glycerin, PEG. Surfactants: polysorbate 80, Cremophor EL. Complexation: cyclodextrins (HP-β-CD). pH adjustment: use acid/base to increase ionization (ionic form more water-soluble). Salt formation: adjust API to salt form

Allowed when no FDA-approved veterinary product exists or is not available. Must have valid veterinarian-client-patient relationship (VCPR). Cannot copy commercial products. Flavoring to improve palatability is acceptable. FDCA extraterritoriality provisions apply across state lines

cards.front: Ophthalmic compounding: tonicity and pH requirements
cards.back: Ophthalmic preparations must be: isotonic (approximately 0.9% NaCl equivalent, 280–300 mOsm/L), sterile, free of particles, appropriate pH (6.5–7.4 preferred). Buffered with phosphate or borate buffers. BUD per USP <797>: Category 1 or Category 2 depending on processing

Must have DEA registration if compounding Schedule II–V substances. Compounded preparations must be derived from FDA-approved bulk APIs (scheduled or otherwise). Cannot compound CIII-V for office use in large quantities without patient-specific Rx. State-specific rules vary

cards.front: Gowning requalification: why annual is insufficient for sterile compounding
cards.back: USP <797> (2023 revision): gloved fingertip sampling required initially (before personnel begin compounding independently) and media fill testing. Media fill tests: at least annually for Category 2 operations; after significant changes. Surface and air monitoring ongoing. Ongoing education required

Voluntary accreditation program for compounding pharmacies demonstrating compliance with USP <795>, <797>, <800>. Requires rigorous on-site inspection. Not mandatory but signals quality to prescribers and payers. 503B outsourcing facilities may seek ISO certification instead

cards.front: Incompatibility types in compounding: physical vs chemical vs therapeutic
cards.back: Physical: precipitation, phase separation (mixing oil/water without emulsifier), color change. Chemical: hydrolysis (aspirin in water), oxidation (vitamin C), photodegradation (nifedipine, nitroprusside). Therapeutic: antagonism or additive toxicity from combined ingredients

Protect from light: nitroprusside, nifedipine, amphotericin B, riboflavin, vitamin A, metronidazole IV. Use amber vials, UV-protective bags, foil overwraps. Store in light-protected areas. Label 'Protect from light.' USP light transmission specifications for amber glass

Laminar airflow workbenches (LAFW) and biological safety cabinets (BSC) must be certified by a certified technician (CETA or equivalent): at initial installation, after any relocation, after repairs/filter changes, and every 6 months ongoing. Velocity and filter integrity tested

Displacement value (DV) = mass of drug displacing 1 g of cocoa butter. If DV = 0.5, then 2 g drug displaces 1 g of base. Adjusted base weight = total suppository weight − (drug weight / DV). Critical for accurate dosing in fatty base suppositories

cards.front: Preservatives in ophthalmic preparations: example and concern
cards.back: Benzalkonium chloride (BAK): most common ophthalmic preservative — antimicrobial. Concern: epithelial toxicity with chronic use (contact lens incompatibility, dry eye). Preservative-free unit dose vials preferred for glaucoma patients, contact lens wearers, severe dry eye
